coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on “antenna effect” for discriminating leucine enantiomers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Haixia
Ma, Wenming
Liao, Xuan
Ren, Cuiling
Chen, Hongli
coredata.dc:description: D-Leucine is a potential biomarker for diagnosing diabetes, therefore detection of D-Leu and discrimination of Leu enantiomers are of great importance. Herein, a new fluorescent nanosensor Eu-DLSiNPs was synthesized at room temperature. Based on the “antenna effect”, a chiral ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC was obtained by simple mixing with oxytetracycline (OTC). This new sensing system showed two distinctive emission peaks at 480 nm and 616 nm, respectively. The addition of D-Leu can cause the fluorescence intensity decreased at 480 nm and enhanced at 616 nm, while L-Leu has no significant effect on the emission of the nanoprobe, so the ratiometric discrimination of Leu enantiomers can be realized. The mechanism of the enantiomeric fluorescence recognition was systematically studied. This work provides a new strategy for the recognition of leucine enantiomers and will paved a new way for discriminating other important chiral compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral recognition
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Leucine enantiomers
Silicon nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC system and typical fluorescence analysis procedures for chiral recognition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image; (B) particle size distribution histogram; (C) FT-IR spectrum and (D) XPS survey spectrum of Eu-DLSiNPs.
Chiral recognition ability of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC towards amino acid enantiomers (the concentration of Asp is 3 mM, other amino acids are 10 mM).

Fluorescence spectra of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC after the addition of different concentrations of (A) D-Leu and (B) L-Leu; (C) Linear relationship between F616/F480 and the concentration of D-Leu; (D) Relationship between F616/F480 and the concentration of L-Leu.

(A) The relationship between relative fluorescence intensity F0/F (480 nm) and the concentration of D-Leu before and after the addition of D-Leu to Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC; (B) UV–Vis spectra; (C) The fluorescence decay curves (480 nm); (D) The fluorescence decay curves (616 nm) before and after adding L/D-Leu to Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC.
Fluorescence spectra of (A) Eu-DLSiNPs; (B) DLSiNPs; (C) DLSiNPs + OTC; (D) Eu3++OTC and (E) OTC before and after the addition of L/D-Leu.
Fluorescence spectra of (A) Eu-DSiNPs; (B)Eu-LSiNPs; (C) Eu-DSiNPs/OTC and (D) Eu-LSiNPs/OTC before and after the addition of L/D-Leu.
Schematic diagram of the construction of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC and its application in leucine enantiomers recognition.

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on “antenna effect” for discriminating leucine enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Special Function Materials and Structure Design (MOE), Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Special Function Materials and Structure Design (MOE)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Special Function Materials and Structure Design (MOE), Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new fluorescent nanosensor Eu-DLSiNPs was synthesized at room temperature, based on the “antenna effect”, a chiral ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe Eu-DLSiNPa/OTC can be obtained by simply mix with oxytetracycline (OTC), which can be used as a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for discriminating leucine enantiomers.

D-Leucine is a potential biomarker for diagnosing diabetes, therefore detection of D-Leu and discrimination of Leu enantiomers are of great importance. Herein, a new fluorescent nanosensor Eu-DLSiNPs was synthesized at room temperature. Based on the “antenna effect”, a chiral ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC was obtained by simple mixing with oxytetracycline (OTC). This new sensing system showed two distinctive emission peaks at 480 nm and 616 nm, respectively. The addition of D-Leu can cause the fluorescence intensity decreased at 480 nm and enhanced at 616 nm, while L-Leu has no significant effect on the emission of the nanoprobe, so the ratiometric discrimination of Leu enantiomers can be realized. The mechanism of the enantiomeric fluorescence recognition was systematically studied. This work provides a new strategy for the recognition of leucine enantiomers and will paved a new way for discriminating other important chiral compounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the main component of protein, amino acids are one of the basic substances that sustain life activities [1]. With the exception of glycine, all amino acids are chiral and their two enantiomers exhibit essential and diverse biological functions [2,3]. Leucine (Leu) is an essential amino acid that is synthesized in plants and microorganisms and has a unique ability to stimulate muscle protein synthesis [4,5]. The enantiomers of Leu have some different effects, such as L-Leu has the ability to stimulate protein synthesis in RLC-16 hepatocytes [6], whereas D-Leu does not have this effect. D-Leu has been shown to be potent anti-seizure effects [7]. In addition, higher levels of D-Leu were found in the nails of diabetic patients compared to healthy volunteers [8], indicating that D-Leu is a potential biomarker for the diagnosis of diabetes. Therefore, the recognition of Leu enantiomers and the detection of D-Leu are of great importance.

A number of methods have been developed for the recognition of Leucine enantiomers, including fluorescent methods [9,10], colorimetric methods [11], capillary electrophoresis [12] and electrochemical methods [13]. Among these methods, enantioselective fluorescence recognition of amino acid enantiomers is a hot topic of research in recent years due to its numerous advantages, such as the simple operation, high sensitivity and multiple detection modes [14]. For example, Hormozi Jangi et al. used N-doped CDs as fluorescent probe to achieve fluorescent recognition L/D-Leu [9].

Compared with single emission fluorescent probes, the ratiometric fluorescence probe can effectively reduce or eliminate the interference caused by external factors such as fluctuations in the excitation light source, changes in the local environment and probe concentration, thus improve the detection accuracy [15]. Until now, some ratiometric fluorescence probes for chiral recognition have been reported. For example, Wei et al. synthesized a β-cyclodextrin functionalized Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots (β-CD-Mn-ZnS QDs), which exhibited two emission centers at 430 nm and 598 nm. The fluorescent intensity of β-CD-Mn-ZnS QDs responds differently to tryptophan enantiomers, so that ratiometric recognition of tryptophan enantiomers can be achieved [16]. Fahimi-Kashani et al. synthesized a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe (CTAB-QDs/BCDs) for the enantioselective detection of tryptophan by combinning cetyltrimethylammonium bromide -induced supramolecular assemblies of CdTe quantum dots (CTAB-QDs) with blue fluorescent carbon dots [17]. Chang et al. used hydrothermal method to prepare a new type of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with two emission centers (440 nm/542 nm). Due to the different binding free energy of L/D-lysine (L/D-Lys) and CDs, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm could be enhanced by L-Lys, while the fluorescence intensity at 542 nm remained unchanged; the fluorescence intensity of both emission peaks did not change significantly after the addition of D-Lys, indicating that the CDs can be used as ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for dicriminating lysine enantiomers[18]. Jafar-Nezhad Ivrigh et al. have constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs) and mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots (MPA-QDs), that enables visualized ratiometric recognition of the enantiomers of histidine and arginine [19]. Jiang et al. have constructed a multiple emissions composite luminescent probe Eu-BTB@CQDs, which can be used as a ratiometric luminescent sensor for recognizing lysine enantiomers [20].

In comparison to these nanoprobes, fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) is an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial with promising applications in chiral recognition [21], bioimaging [22], drug delivery [23], temperature determination [24] and anti-counterfeiting [25] due to their superior optical properties, abundant surface functional groups, excellent biocompatibility and simple preparation method. Lanthanide complexes have been widely studied in the field of fluorescence detection because of excellent luminous performance and energy transfer efficiency [26].

Lanthanide metal ions have very weak fluorescence emission intensity, when they are coordinated with organic ligands containing chromophores, the ligands absorb energy from the outside world and then transfer energy to the lanthanide metal ions through a complex energy transfer process, which then sensitises its luminescence and enhances its fluorescence emission, a process known as the “antenna effect”. Due to their simplicity of preparation and good sensitization effect on europium ions, β-diketonate complexes are the focus of the study of lanthanide complexes [27]. Furthermore, Eu3+-β-diketonate complexes have been intensively studied due to their inherent extremely sharp emission peaks and high quantum efficiency. For example, Li et al. used lanthanide β‑diketonate complex functionalized poly(ionicliquid)s/SiO2 microsphere as a fluorescent probe for the determination of bovine hemoglobin [28]. To the best of our knowledge, up to now, ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for Leu enantioselective determination have not been reported. Therefore, the development of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on SiNPs and Eu3+-β-diketonate complexes for the fluorescence recognition of Leu enantiomers is of great significant.

In this work, an achiral fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (Eu-DLSiNPs) were synthesized by a one-step process using DL-methionine (DL-Met), N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (DAMO), Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) and mercaptoacetic acid (TGA) as raw materials at room temperature. Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a chiral molecule with a β‑diketonate structure, the fluorescent intensity of Eu3+ can be enhanced by the “antenna effect”. On the basis of this, we have proposed for the first time a chiral ratiometric nanoprobe (Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC) for the recognition of Leu enantiomers (Scheme 1 ).

N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (DAMO) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Ltd. Europium(Ⅲ)nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O), oxytetracycline (OTC), DL-Methionine, L/D-Methionine, L/D-glutamic acid, L/D-glutamine, L/D-alanine, L/D-proline and other amino acids were bought from Adamas Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the Eu-DLSiNPs were measured using a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, America) spectroscopy was conducted on a Nicolet Nexus 670 FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were measured using an Axis Supra X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. UV–visible absorption spectra were performed on a TU-1901 double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescent (FL) spectra were carried out using an RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a Xe lamp (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Time-resolved FL spectra were obtained using a time-correlated single-photo counting (TCSPC) system from an FL 920 spectrometer. Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were measured on a JASCO J1500 circular dichroism spectrometer.

0.0597 g of DL-methionine was dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water, then 6 mL of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (2 mM) and 30 μL of mercaptoacetic acid was added and stirred for 5 min to dissolve completely. Finally, 1 mL of N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine was added and stirred for 48 h. The resulting reaction solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected and dialyzed (0–500 Da) for 24 h, and the prepared Eu-DLSiNPs were stored at 4℃ for use

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC system and typical fluorescence analysis procedures for chiral recognition
ce:section.ce:para: The Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC system was obtained by dispersing 200 μL Eu-DLSiNPs solution into HEPES (10 mM, pH=7.10) buffer solution containing OTC (60 μL, 1 mM). Then different concentrations of L- or D-Leu solutions were added to Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC. The total volume of the mixed solution was kept at 3.0 mL. After mixing evenly, the solution was incubated in a water bath at 50℃ for 25 min, and its fluorescence spectrum was recorded.

Compound amino acid injection (18AA) was diluted tenfold and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, a volume of 100 μL was then transferred into centrifuge tubes, to which varying concentrations of D-leucine solution were separately added. Finally, their fluorescence spectra were recorded. Throughout the detection process, the total volume of the solution was maintained at 3 mL.

The morphology and size of Eu-DLSiNPs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, Eu-SiNPs are spherical, their average diameter is calculated to be 2.3 nm according to the particle size distribution (Fig. 1B). The surface functional groups of Eu-DLSiNPs were identified by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1C). The absorption bands between 3000–3650 cm−1 are characteristic absorption bands of –OH and –NH [29]. The absorption peaks at 2925 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H [30]. The strong signals at 1600 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibration of N-H bond [31], and the absorption peaks in the 1350–1470 cm−1 range are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N [32,33]. The absorption peaks at 1036 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 is attributed to Si-O-Si and Si-O-C, respectively [34]. Si-O-Si group shows Si-O stretching at 930 cm−1, and the two peaks centered at 776 and 692 cm−1 belonges to N-H wagging vibration and secondary amine vibration, respectively [35]. The FTIR spectra preliminarilyindicates there are abundant –OH, –NH2 groups which endow them with good water solubility.

The valence states of the elements within Eu-DLSiNPs were analyzed by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1D) confirms the elemental composition of Eu-DLSiNPs are C, N, O, Si, S, and Eu. In the high-resolution of C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S1A), the peaks at 284.1, 284.7, 285.5, and 287.8 eV can be assigned to C-Si, C-C, C-N, C=O, respectively [36,37]. The three peaks in the XPS spectrum of N 1 s are located at 398.3 eV (N-C), 399.5 eV (C-N-C) and 399.6 eV (Si-N-C) (Fig. S1B) [38,39]. As shown in Fig. S1C, the three peaks appeared in the O 1 s spectrum is Si-O (530.3 eV), C-OH (531.1 eV), and C-O (532.2 eV) [40,41]. The Si 2p peak at 101.6 ev, 102.0 ev and 102.6 ev are assigned to Si-C, Si-N and Si-O, respectively (Fig. S1D) [42]. The two peaks at 163.2 ev, 167.8 eV in the S2p XPS spectrum suggests the presence of C-S-C, −SO2, −SO3 (Fig. S1E) in Eu-DLSiNPs [43]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of Eu 3d exhibited two peaks at 1134.7 ev and 1164.7 eV, corresponding to Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2 of Eu (III) ion (Fig. S1F) [44]. These results prove the successful preparation of Eu-DLSiNPs and further confirmes Eu-DLSiNPs possess abundant functional groups on the surfaces, which is in consistent with FT-IR spectrum.

Stability of the detection system is favorable for sample analysis, hence the stability of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC under different conditions is also investigated. As shown in Fig. S2A, the value F616/F480(F616 and F480 is the photoluminescent intensity of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC at 616 nm and 480 nm, respectively) did not change significantly after UV light irradiation for 1 h, which prove Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC is resistant to photobleaching. This value also kept constant when the temperature increased from 30 ℃ to 55 ℃ (Fig. S2B). In summary, Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC has good stability. Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescent signals of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC from different batches were also tested. As shown in Fig. S2C, F616/F480 of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC from five different batches did not change significantly, demonstrating Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC in each batch has good stability, ensure its detection performance.

The enantioselective recognition capacity of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC towards some common amino acid enantiomers was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 , compared with other amino acids, Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC only shows significant chiral recognition ability for Leu enantiomers under the same conditions. Some common anions have negligible effects on the fluorescence signal of the probe (Fig. S3).

The discrimination performance of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC to Leu enantiomers were further investigated. To obtain the best enantioselective recognition performance for Leu enantiomers, the influence of concentration of OTC, pH, reaction time and temperature on the discrimination capacity of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC to Leu enantiomers were studied. As shown in Fig. S4, the value of (F616/F480)D/(F616/F480)L ((F616/F480)D and (F616/F480)L is the value of F616/F480 after the addition of D-Leu and L-Leu, respectively) can be affected by the concentration of OTC, and reaches its maximum when the concentration of OTC is 20 μM. The influence of pH was investigated in the range of 6.8 ∼ 8.0 with an interval of 0.3, the value of (F616/F480)D/(F616/F480)L is maximal at pH 7.1. Reaction time was investigated between 15 and 40 min, and the recognition process achieves equilibrium at 25 min. The value of (F616/F480)D/(F616/F480)L showsthetrendofinitialincreasing and thendecreasing, reachingthepeakat 50 ℃.To sum up, the best detection condition for discriminating Leu enantiomers is 20 μM OTC, pH 7.1, incubation temperature and time is 50 ℃ and 25 min, respectively.

The recognition ability of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC for Leu enantiomers was investigated under the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 A, as the concentration of D-Leu increases, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC decreases at 480 nm while enhances at 616 nm. However, Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC shows no significant response to L-Leu (Fig. 3B), indicating that Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC could achieve ratiometric discrimination of Leu enantiomers. The value of F616/F480 is linear relate to the concentration of D-Leu in the range of 1–18 mM with a detection limit of 0.14 mM (Fig. 3C). Conversely, F616/F480 remained almost unchanged as the concentration of L-Leu increased, (Fig. 3D). The spiking experiments showed that the recoveries of D-Leu at three different spiking concentration ranged from 103.2 % to 103.7 %, the RSD ranged from 0.1 % to 0.3 % (Table S1). The reliability of this method was further verified by CD spectroscopy, the experimental results indicate that Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC can serve as an effective probe for distinguishing Leu enantiomers. A typical comparison between Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC and other reported literatures are shown in Table S2, revealing the performance of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC is equivalent or better than reported ones.

The determination of enantiomeric excess (ee=(CL-CD)/(CL+CD)) is important in many fields, including asymmetric catalysis, chiral drugs synthesis and combinatorial chemistry. Therefore, the relationship between ee of D-Leu and F616/F480 of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC was explored in this work. As can be seen in Fig. S5, F616/F480 of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC was linearly correlated with ee of D-Leu in the range of −100 % to 100 %. These results indicate Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC can be used as a fluorescent nanoprobe to predict the ratio of L and D-Leu in their mixtures. The ee of D-Leu was converted into its concentration, it is also linearly fit with the value of F616/F480 (Fig. S5), this slope is almost the same with that in Fig. 3C, further confirming the good enantioselectivity of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC to D-Leu. The applicability of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC in analysis of real samples was explored by compound amino acid injection by standard addition method, the detection results are shown in Table S3, the proposed method can realize qualitative determination of D-Leu in real samples.

In order to understand the chiral recognition mechanism of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC to D/L-Leu, typical Stern–Volmer theory is first adopted. The Stern–Volmer equation can be described as F0/F = 1 + Ksv[D-Leu], where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm without and with the addition of D-Leu, respectively, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant. Fig. 4 A depicts the Stern–Volmer plot analysis, and it can be seen that there is no good linearity between F0/F and D-Leu concentration, suggesting both static quenching and dynamic quenching possibly involved in the interaction between Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC and D-Leu [45]. To deeply understand the recognition mechanism, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC before and after the addition of D/L-Leu were measured, which is one of the most useful strategies to distinguish static and dynamic quenching. As shown in Fig. 4B, the absorption peak at 370 nm red-shifts about 10 nm after the addition of D-Leu, while almost unchanged by the addition of L-Leu, suggesting static quenching happened. Subsequently, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC, which is another useful strategy to distinguish static and dynamic quenching, was measured and summarized in Table S4 and Table S5, respectively. It can be seen that the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC at 480 nm increased (Fig. 4C) and decreased at 616 nm (Fig. 4D) with the addition of D-Leu, while kept constant in the presence of L-Leu, indicating that more D-Leu was bound to Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC [46]. The above experimental results comprehensively prove that the fluorescence quenching of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC by D-Leu is the synergistic effect static quenching and dynamic quenching.

In order to further investigate the chiral recognition mechanism, fluorescent silicon nanoparticles without Eu3+ (DLSiNPs) were synthesized using the same method. The response of Eu-DLSiNPs, DLSiNPs, mixture of DLSiNPs and OTC, mixture of Eu3+ and OTC and OTC solution (300 μM) to Leu enantiomers were examined. The results are shown in Fig. 5 , it was found that they all showed no chiral recognition ability to Leu enantiomers. These results demonstrated that DLSiNPs, Eu3+ and OTC all played an important role in discriminating Leu enantiomers.

Because Met and OTC are chiral molecules, so their role in chiral disrimination of Leu enantiomers were further investigated. Fluorescent silicon nanoparticles Eu-DSiNPs and Eu-LSiNPs were synthesized by using D- and L-Met as the chiral ligand, separately. Their CD signals were found to be mirror symmetric with absorption at 205 nm (Fig. S6), while the absorption peaks of D-Met and L-Met were at 200 nm (Fig. S6B), indicating Eu-DSiNPs and Eu-LSiNPs are chiral. But the fluorescence spectra of Eu-D/LSiNPs did not change significantly before and after the addition of L/D-Leu, implying they showed no enantioselective recognition capacity toward L/D-Leu (Fig. 6 A and B). Then, Eu-DSiNPs/OTC and Eu-LSiNPs/OTC systems were constructed based on Eu-DSiNPs and Eu-LSiNPs, respectively. And their responses to D-Leu and L-Leu is similar to that of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC system (Fig. 6C and D). Therefore, we speculated that D-Met and L-Met retained on the surface of Eu-DLSiNPs, but play little role in the chiral recognition process.

The above experimental results indicate that OTC plays an indispensable role in the chiral recognition process. Because OTC is a chiral molecule, it can endow achiral Eu-DLSiNPs with chirality (Fig. S6C), and the chiral microenvironment provided by OTC was more favorable for Leu enantiomers recognition than chiral Eu-D/LSiNPs [47]. As shown in Fig. S7, the zeta potential for Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC is 5.3 mV, and this value was found to be 5.6 and 7.9 mV after interacting with L-Leu and D-Leu, respectively. This result preliminarily confirms more D-Leu molecules were interact with Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC [48]. Moreover, the particles size and dispersity of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC were almost unchanged in the presence of L-Leu, but after the addition of D-Leu, the particles become more aggregated, which further confirms the stronger interaction between D-Leu and Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC. According to the previous reports, the binding sites on particles surface are more available for D-Leu than L-Leu [9], which had a greater effect on the energy transfer between OTC with DLSiNPs-Eu, resulting in different changes in the fluorescence spectra of Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC, achieved the recognition of D-Leu enantiomers.

A new strategy for ratiometric recognition of Leu enantiomers based on fluorescence spectroscopy by using Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC as a ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe has been developed. The chiral nanoprobe can be simply prepared by using achiral Eu-DLSiNPs mixed with a chiral compound OTC. The mechanism of detection and recognition of Leu enantiomers by the Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC was investigated. The analytical method based on Eu-DLSiNPs/OTC is of practical importance in expanding the methods for recognition Leu enantiomers.

Haixia Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Wenming Ma: Visualization, Validation, Software. Xuan Liao: Conceptualization, Methodology. Cuiling Ren: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hongli Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21874061) and the Science and Technology program of Gansu Province (No. 22JR5RA476).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111274.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of amino acids based on cucurbit[7]uril-mediated three-dimensional gold nanoassemblies
Chin. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liu, H. Zhang, Y. Wang, Y. Zeng, S. Chatterjee, F. Liang, Electrochemical detection of amino acids based on cucurbit[7]uril-mediated three-dimensional gold nanoassemblies, Chin. Chem. Lett., 34 (2023) 107712.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Free amino acid recognition: A bisbinaphthyl-based fluorescent probe with high enantioselectivity
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Y. Zhu, X.D. Wu, S.X. Gu, L. Pu, Free Amino Acid Recognition: A Bisbinaphthyl-Based Fluorescent Probe with High Enantioselectivity, J. Am. Chem. Soc., 141 (2019) 175-181.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral amino acid recognition in water: A BINOL-based fluorescent probe
Chin. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.X. Sun, C.Z. Ni, F.Q. Zhang, Y.Y. Zhu, J. Zeng, S.X. Gu, Chiral amino acid recognition in water: A BINOL-based fluorescent probe, Chin. Chem. Lett., 34 (2023) 108345.
